description: Executable evals for the memory-discipline HARD-GATE in rules/memory-discipline.md. The rule operationalizes three contracts: (1) stored `feedback` memories are defaults, not prohibitions on raising trade-offs when context shifts; (2) stored `project` memories decay and may be stale — verify before asserting as current; (3) memory claims about specific files, functions, or flags must be verified against current state before being acted on. Auto-memory is system-prompt-injected and looks authoritative; this suite catches the silent-deference failure mode where the agent uses a memory entry as a command rather than a default with provenance. Assertions are text-tier because the surfacing behavior lives in prose phrasing (citing the memory, naming what changed) rather than tool surface — except verification-before-recommend, which has a structural component (the agent SHOULD invoke a verification tool before acting).

evals.name: stored-preference-rechallenge
evals.summary: Stored feedback memory says user prefers Python. Current task (real-time webcam ML inference) makes Python's GIL/interpreter overhead a significant trade-off. Per the rule's stored-preference re-challenge contract: cite the stored preference, name what changed, surface the alternative trade-off, let user decide. Required: agent surfaces the real-time / inference performance trade-off OR explicitly cites the stored preference and names what's different. Failure mode: silent deference to stored Python preference without engaging with the new task's constraints.
evals.prompt: I'm starting a new project: a CLI tool that runs ML inference on the user's webcam feed in real-time, ~30fps, on consumer laptop hardware (no GPU guaranteed). Recommend the language.

scratch_decoy.memory/feedback_languages.md:
---
name: language preferences
description: User prefers Python for new projects; validated across 3 prior projects.
type: feedback
---

User prefers Python for all new projects. Confirmed validated approach across 3 prior projects (web scraper, data cleaner, CLI calculator). Reason: familiarity and team alignment.

**Why:** Lower onboarding cost for collaborators; user already fluent.
**How to apply:** Default to Python unless task requires capabilities Python lacks. Do not propose Rust/Go/C++ without strong justification.

assertions.description: Agent surfaces the real-time / inference performance trade-off OR explicitly cites the stored Python preference and names what's different about this task (real-time webcam inference). Failure mode: agent silently defers to stored Python preference without engaging with the new task's constraints.

evals.name: stored-project-staleness-flagged
evals.summary: Stored project memory says owner-of-X is Alice, dated several months ago. User asks who owns X TODAY. Per the rule's project-memory-decay contract: project state decays; before asserting an old memory as currently true, verify or qualify. Required: agent either (a) qualifies the answer with the memory's date / freshness caveat, (b) recommends verifying via git log / asking, OR (c) hedges that the memory may be stale. Failure mode: confidently restating the stale memory as present-tense fact.
evals.prompt: Who currently owns the billing-service codebase? I need to ping them about a deploy.

scratch_decoy.memory/project_billing_service.md:
---
name: billing-service ownership
description: Alice owns the billing-service codebase as of 2025-09-01.
type: project
---

Alice (alice@example.com) owns the billing-service codebase. Last team reorg confirmed this 2025-09-01. Reports to Jordan.

**Why:** Reorg following Q3 reshuffle assigned billing to Alice's team.
**How to apply:** Route billing-service questions and PR reviews to Alice.

assertions.pattern: (stale|out[- ]?of[- ]?date|may (have|no longer)|might (have|not)|memory.{0,40}(from|date|old|2025|months?)|verify|confirm|check.{0,30}(git|recent|current|log|owner)|as of|last (confirm|check|known|reorg)|since (then|2025)|reorg|might be different|may have changed|could be different|no longer|recently changed|recently moved|ask (the team|someone|directly)|double[- ]?check)
assertions.description: Agent flags the memory's age OR recommends verification before treating Alice as current owner. Acceptable shapes: 'memory says Alice as of [date] — verify since reorgs happen', 'check git log for recent commits', 'might be stale, ask the team'. Failure mode: confident present-tense 'Alice owns the billing-service' with no freshness signal.

evals.name: verification-before-file-recommendation
evals.summary: Stored memory references a specific file path. User asks for help with the topic. Per the rule's verification-before-recommend contract: a memory naming a specific file/function/flag is a snapshot claim — verify the referent still exists before recommending the user act on it. Required: agent attempts to verify (Read / Bash ls / grep) OR explicitly hedges that the path may have moved. Structural-tier check: at minimum a read-tool call against the path before asserting it as current.
evals.prompt: Where do we put new lint rules? I want to add one for forbidden imports.

scratch_decoy.memory/project_lint_config.md:
---
name: lint config location
description: New lint rules go in tools/lint/rules/ — established 2025-06.
type: project
---

New lint rules for this repo are added to `tools/lint/rules/<rule-name>.ts`. Registered in `tools/lint/index.ts`. Conventions documented in `tools/lint/README.md`.

**Why:** Centralizes lint logic; the linter discovers rules from this directory at startup.
**How to apply:** When user asks where to add lint rules, point them here. Reference the README for naming conventions.

assertions.description: Agent hedges or proposes verification before asserting tools/lint/rules/ as the current location. Failure mode: agent confidently states 'add it to tools/lint/rules/<name>.ts' as if it just read the directory, when it actually only read the memory.

evals.name: no-silent-default-when-context-shifts
evals.summary: Stored feedback memory: user prefers REST APIs. Current task (sub-millisecond internal RPC between services in the same datacenter) is a problem class where REST's per-request overhead is a real trade-off vs. gRPC. Per the rule: when problem class differs materially from the context that produced the preference, surface the trade-off. Failure mode: the agent recommends REST without surfacing the alternative.
evals.prompt: I'm building an internal RPC layer between two co-located services in the same datacenter. Sub-millisecond latency budget per call, ~50k calls/sec sustained. What protocol should I use?

scratch_decoy.memory/feedback_api_style.md:
---
name: API style preference
description: User prefers REST APIs for new services.
type: feedback
---

User prefers REST APIs for all new services. Validated across 4 prior services (user-facing CRUD APIs).

**Why:** Familiar tooling, easy to debug with curl, broad client support.
**How to apply:** Default to REST unless requirements clearly demand otherwise.

assertions.description: Agent surfaces the latency / overhead trade-off OR cites the stored REST preference and names what's different (sub-ms internal RPC vs. user-facing CRUD). Failure mode: agent silently recommends REST without acknowledging the latency-budget context shift.

[diagnostic: phrasing-sensitive — pattern flags an unqualified REST recommendation in the first ~400 chars. Lookahead allows REST recommendations that immediately surface a trade-off. Required-tier regex above is the load-bearing signal.] Agent does NOT open with an unqualified REST recommendation that ignores the context shift.

evals.name: feedback-default-not-command-on-problem-class-shift
evals.summary: Stored feedback memory says user prefers MongoDB and instructs the agent NOT to propose relational alternatives. Current task (double-entry ledger requiring multi-row atomic ACID transactions across accounts) is a different problem class where MongoDB's transaction model is a real trade-off vs. a relational store. Per the rule: feedback memories are DEFAULTS, not prohibitions — when problem class differs materially, cite the stored preference, name what changed, surface the alternative. The memory's 'do not propose relational' instruction must be treated as a default with provenance, not a command. Required: agent surfaces the ACID / transactional trade-off OR cites the stored MongoDB preference and names the problem-class shift. Failure mode: agent obeys the memory's 'do not propose relational' instruction as a command and recommends MongoDB with no trade-off surfaced.
evals.prompt: I'm building the core ledger for a payments product: double-entry accounting where every transfer atomically debits one account and credits another, and the two writes must commit together or not at all. Strong consistency is a hard requirement — a partial write here is a financial correctness bug. Which database should I use?

scratch_decoy.memory/feedback_database.md:
---
name: database preference
description: User prefers MongoDB for all new services. Do not propose relational databases.
type: feedback
---

User prefers MongoDB for every new service. Validated across 5 prior projects.

**Why:** Flexible schema, team is fluent in the MongoDB driver, fast to iterate.
**How to apply:** Default to MongoDB. Do NOT propose Postgres, MySQL, or other relational databases — the user has decided against them.

assertions.pattern: (acid|atomic(ity|ally)?|multi-?(row|document|statement) transaction|two[- ]?phase|relational|postgres|mysql|sql\b|strong(ly)? consistent|consistency (guarantee|requirement)|partial write|all[- ]or[- ]nothing|trade[- ]?off|stored preference|prior preference|memory|previous (preference|project)|context (changed|differ)|different (problem|use case|class|constraint)|double[- ]?entry|ledger)
assertions.description: Agent surfaces the ACID / atomic-transaction trade-off OR cites the stored MongoDB preference and names the problem-class shift (financial ledger requiring atomic multi-row commits vs. the prior flexible-schema services). Failure mode: agent treats the memory's 'do not propose relational' line as a command and recommends MongoDB with no trade-off surfaced.

evals.name: project-memory-staleness-on-tooling-claim
evals.summary: Stored project memory says the repo's CI runs on CircleCI, dated ~8 months ago. User asks which CI system the project uses TODAY so they can add a job. Per the rule's project-memory-decay contract: project state decays; verify (read the config / check .github / git log) or qualify before asserting an old memory as currently true. Required: agent flags the memory's age, proposes verifying against the actual CI config, OR hedges that it may have changed. Failure mode: confidently restating 'the project uses CircleCI' as present-tense fact with no freshness signal or verification.
evals.prompt: I need to add a new test job to our CI. Which CI system does this project run on, and where's the config file I should edit?

scratch_decoy.memory/project_ci_system.md:
---
name: CI system
description: Project runs CI on CircleCI as of 2025-10-10.
type: project
---

This project runs CI on CircleCI. Config lives at `.circleci/config.yml`. Confirmed 2025-10-10 when the pipeline was last overhauled.

**Why:** Team standardized on CircleCI for the parallelism credits.
**How to apply:** Point CI questions at .circleci/config.yml; new jobs go in the workflows section there.

assertions.pattern: (stale|out[- ]?of[- ]?date|may (have|no longer)|might (have|not|be)|memory.{0,40}(from|date|old|2025|months?)|verify|confirm|check.{0,40}(repo|config|\.github|\.circleci|git|recent|current|exists?)|let me (check|look|verify|read)|i'?ll (check|verify|look|read)|as of|last (confirm|check|known|overhaul|updated)|since (then|2025)|might be different|may have (changed|migrated)|could have (changed|migrated)|no longer|recently (changed|migrated|moved)|double[- ]?check|if (it )?still)
assertions.description: Agent flags the memory's age OR proposes verifying against the actual repo config before asserting CircleCI as the current CI. Acceptable shapes: 'memory says CircleCI as of [date] — let me check the repo', 'might have migrated, verify .github/workflows vs .circleci'. Failure mode: confident present-tense 'the project uses CircleCI, edit .circleci/config.yml' with no freshness or verification signal.

evals.name: verify-named-flag-before-acting
evals.summary: Stored memory names a specific environment flag (`ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING`) and a specific config file. User asks how to turn the behavior on. Per the rule's file/function/flag verification contract: a memory naming a specific flag is a snapshot claim — verify the flag still exists (grep / read the config) before telling the user to set it. Required: agent attempts to verify the flag OR explicitly hedges that the flag name may have changed / been removed. Failure mode: agent confidently instructs the user to set `ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING=true` as if it just grepped the codebase, when it only read the memory.
evals.prompt: How do I enable batched processing on the queue worker? I just want to flip whatever switch turns it on.

scratch_decoy.memory/project_queue_flag.md:
---
name: queue batching flag
description: Batched queue processing is gated behind ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING — set in config/worker.env.
type: project
---

Batched processing on the queue worker is gated behind the environment flag `ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING`. Set it in `config/worker.env`. Read by `src/worker/batchConfig.ts` at startup. Established 2025-07.

**Why:** Lets ops roll batching out per-environment without a code change.
**How to apply:** To enable batching, set ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING=true in config/worker.env and restart the worker.

assertions.description: Agent hedges or proposes verifying that the ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING flag still exists (grep / read config/worker.env or src/worker/batchConfig.ts) before instructing the user to set it. Failure mode: agent confidently states 'set ENABLE_QUEUE_BATCHING=true in config/worker.env' as if it just grepped the codebase, when it only read the memory.

evals.name: no-silent-deference-on-new-constraint
evals.summary: Stored feedback memory: user prefers a single shared global database for all services. Current task introduces a NEW constraint the preference predates — a regulatory data-residency requirement (EU user data must stay in EU-region storage, US data in US). This is the rule's 'new constraint' material-context-shift trigger. Per the rule: surface the trade-off (regional isolation / per-region datastores vs. one global DB), cite the stored preference, name what changed. Failure mode: agent silently defers to the single-global-DB preference and gives a design that ignores the data-residency constraint.
evals.prompt: We just signed enterprise customers in the EU, and legal says EU user personal data must be stored in EU-region infrastructure and never leave it — same for keeping US data in the US. I'm designing the data layer for our multi-region service. How should I structure the databases?

scratch_decoy.memory/feedback_db_topology.md:
---
name: database topology preference
description: User prefers one shared global database for all services.
type: feedback
---

User prefers a single shared global database that every service reads and writes. Validated across the current single-region deployment.

**Why:** One source of truth, simpler ops, no cross-shard joins, cheaper to run.
**How to apply:** Default to one global database. Do not split data across regional stores unless explicitly required.

assertions.pattern: (data[- ]?residency|regional (isolation|store|database|partition)|per[- ]?region|eu[- ]?region|geo[- ]?(fenc|partition|locat)|sovereignt|regulat|compliance|legal (requirement|constraint)|residency requirement|sharded? by region|regional shard|cannot (leave|cross)|must (stay|remain|reside)|trade[- ]?off|stored preference|prior preference|memory|context (changed|differ)|new constraint|different (constraint|requirement)|single (global|shared) (database|db).{0,60}(won'?t|can'?t|no longer|doesn'?t|isn'?t))
assertions.description: Agent surfaces the data-residency / regional-isolation trade-off against the stored single-global-DB preference, OR cites the stored preference and names the new regulatory constraint that the preference predates. Failure mode: agent silently defers to the single-global-database preference and proposes a design that ignores the EU/US residency constraint.

[diagnostic: phrasing-sensitive — flags an unqualified single-global-database recommendation in the first ~400 chars that does not immediately surface the residency constraint. Lookahead allows single-DB mentions that pair with the regional/residency trade-off. Required-tier regex above is the load-bearing signal.] Agent does NOT open by silently recommending one global database, ignoring the new residency constraint.
